examples.explanation: Start: knapsack(0, 5, 2). Item 0: weight=2, value=5, copies=2. Option 1: take copy: value=5 + knapsack(0, 3, 1). Recursive: knapsack(0, 3, 1): take copy: value=5 + knapsack(0, 1, 0). Recursive: knapsack(0, 1, 0): copies=0, skip to item 1: knapsack(1, 1, 1). Item 1: weight=3 > 1, skip: knapsack(2, 1, 0) -> base case return 0. So knapsack(0, 1, 0)=0, thus take=5+0=5. Skip option: knapsack(1, 3, 1). Item 1: take copy: value=6 + knapsack(1, 0, 0) -> base case return 0, so take=6. Skip: knapsack(2, 3, 0) -> 0. Max=6. So knapsack(0, 3, 1)=max(5,6)=6. Thus first take path: 5+6=11. Option 2: skip item 0: knapsack(1, 5, 1). Item 1: take copy: 6 + knapsack(1, 2, 0) -> skip to end: 0, so take=6. Skip: knapsack(2,5,0)=0. Max=6. Overall max(11,6)=11.

Start: knapsack(0, 4, 1). Item 0: weight=1, value=2, copies=1. Take: 2 + knapsack(0, 3, 0). Recursive: knapsack(0, 3, 0): copies=0, skip to item 1: knapsack(1, 3, 2). Item 1: weight=2, value=3, copies=2. Take copy: 3 + knapsack(1, 1, 1). Recursive: knapsack(1, 1, 1): weight=2 > 1, skip: knapsack(2, 1, 0)=0. So take=3. Skip: knapsack(2,1,0)=0. Max=3. Thus knapsack(1,3,2)=max(take:3+3=6, skip:knapsack(2,3,0)=0)=6. So first take path: 2+6=8. Skip item 0: knapsack(1,4,2). Item 1: take copy: 3 + knapsack(1,2,1). Recursive: knapsack(1,2,1): take copy: 3 + knapsack(1,0,0)=3. Skip: knapsack(2,2,0)=0. Max=3. So take=3+3=6. Skip: knapsack(2,4,0)=0. Max=6. Overall max(8,6)=8.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Try all possible combinations of items by recursively including or excluding each item within its quantity limit. This explores the complete solution space but becomes impractical for larger inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Bounded Knapsack with Binary Splitting via Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete solution exploring all combinations of items with quantity limits using recursion.
ladders.explanation: The bounded knapsack problem involves selecting items with given weights, values, and quantity limits to maximize total value without exceeding a weight capacity. The brute force recursion approach explores every possible combination by recursively deciding whether to include or exclude each item, respecting its quantity limit. This is achieved by treating each item as having multiple copies, which can be represented through binary splitting—where an item with quantity q is considered as log2(q) separate items in the recursion, each representing a power-of-two subset of the original quantity. The intuition is to exhaustively search the solution space: at each step, we either take a copy of the current item (if weight allows and copies remain) or skip it, moving to the next item. This ensures all combinations are checked, but it leads to exponential time complexity due to the branching factor of 2 per item copy. The step-by-step algorithm: define a recursive function knapsack(i, remainingWeight, remainingCopies) where i is the current item index, remainingWeight is the available capacity, and remainingCopies is the number of copies left for the current item. Base cases: if i exceeds the number of items or remainingWeight <= 0, return 0. Recursive cases: if remainingCopies > 0 and item weight <= remainingWeight, consider taking a copy (value + knapsack(i, remainingWeight - weight, remainingCopies - 1)) or skipping to next item (knapsack(i+1, remainingWeight, originalCopies of next item)). Return the maximum of these options. Time complexity is O(2^n) where n is the total number of item copies after binary splitting, as each copy leads to two recursive calls. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. Connection to L1: This full problem relies on the key subroutine of recursive decision-making at each item copy. Common mistakes include not handling quantity limits correctly, leading to infinite recursion, or missing base cases. Real code pattern: def knapsack(i, cap, copies): if i >= n or cap <= 0: return 0; if copies[i] > 0 and weights[i] <= cap: take = values[i] + knapsack(i, cap - weights[i], copies[i] - 1); skip = knapsack(i+1, cap, original_copies[i+1]); return max(take, skip); else: return knapsack(i+1, cap, original_copies[i+1]).

examples.trace: Start: knapsack(0, 5, 2). Item 0: weight=2, value=5, copies=2. Option 1: take copy: value=5 + knapsack(0, 3, 1). Recursive: knapsack(0, 3, 1): take copy: value=5 + knapsack(0, 1, 0). Recursive: knapsack(0, 1, 0): copies=0, skip to item 1: knapsack(1, 1, 1). Item 1: weight=3 > 1, skip: knapsack(2, 1, 0) -> base case return 0. So knapsack(0, 1, 0)=0, thus take=5+0=5. Skip option: knapsack(1, 3, 1). Item 1: take copy: value=6 + knapsack(1, 0, 0) -> base case return 0, so take=6. Skip: knapsack(2, 3, 0) -> 0. Max=6. So knapsack(0, 3, 1)=max(5,6)=6. Thus first take path: 5+6=11. Option 2: skip item 0: knapsack(1, 5, 1). Item 1: take copy: 6 + knapsack(1, 2, 0) -> skip to end: 0, so take=6. Skip: knapsack(2,5,0)=0. Max=6. Overall max(11,6)=11.

Start: knapsack(0, 4, 1). Item 0: weight=1, value=2, copies=1. Take: 2 + knapsack(0, 3, 0). Recursive: knapsack(0, 3, 0): copies=0, skip to item 1: knapsack(1, 3, 2). Item 1: weight=2, value=3, copies=2. Take copy: 3 + knapsack(1, 1, 1). Recursive: knapsack(1, 1, 1): weight=2 > 1, skip: knapsack(2, 1, 0)=0. So take=3. Skip: knapsack(2,1,0)=0. Max=3. Thus knapsack(1,3,2)=max(take:3+3=6, skip:knapsack(2,3,0)=0)=6. So first take path: 2+6=8. Skip item 0: knapsack(1,4,2). Item 1: take copy: 3 + knapsack(1,2,1). Recursive: knapsack(1,2,1): take copy: 3 + knapsack(1,0,0)=3. Skip: knapsack(2,2,0)=0. Max=3. So take=3+3=6. Skip: knapsack(2,4,0)=0. Max=6. Overall max(8,6)=8.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Decision for Item Inclusion with Quantity Limits
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that decides whether to take a copy of an item or skip it.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that handles the decision-making at each step in the bounded knapsack problem. It takes parameters: current item index, remaining weight capacity, and remaining copies for the current item. The intuition is to explore two possibilities at each call: either include a copy of the current item (if possible) or move to the next item. This mimics a depth-first search through the solution space. The step-by-step algorithm: function knapsack(i, cap, copies). Base cases: if i >= number of items or cap <= 0, return 0. Recursive cases: if copies > 0 and weight[i] <= cap, compute take = value[i] + knapsack(i, cap - weight[i], copies - 1) (include a copy and recurse with reduced capacity and copies), and skip = knapsack(i+1, cap, original_copies[i+1]) (skip to next item with its full copies). Return max(take, skip). If copies == 0 or weight[i] > cap, simply return knapsack(i+1, cap, original_copies[i+1]). Time complexity for this sub-routine in isolation is O(2^m) where m is the number of recursive calls, but it's part of the larger exponential complexity. Space complexity is O(m) due to recursion depth. Connection to L0: This sub-routine is repeatedly called in the full solution to build up the maximum value. Connection to L2: It relies on the core logic of branching decisions. Common mistakes: forgetting to decrement copies when taking an item, or not resetting copies when moving to the next item. Real code pattern: def knapsack(i, cap, copies): if i >= n or cap <= 0: return 0; if copies > 0 and weights[i] <= cap: take = values[i] + knapsack(i, cap - weights[i], copies - 1); skip = knapsack(i+1, cap, original_copies[i+1]); return max(take, skip); else: return knapsack(i+1, cap, original_copies[i+1]).

examples.input: i=0, cap=5, copies=2, weights=[2], values=[5], n=1
examples.trace: Call knapsack(0,5,2). Base: i=0<1, cap=5>0. copies=2>0 and weight=2<=5. Take: value=5 + knapsack(0,3,1). Recursive: knapsack(0,3,1): copies=1>0 and weight=2<=3. Take: 5 + knapsack(0,1,0). Recursive: knapsack(0,1,0): copies=0, so else: knapsack(1,1,original_copies[1]) but i=1>=n=1, base return 0. So take=5+0=5. Skip: knapsack(1,3,original_copies[1])=0. Max=5. Thus knapsack(0,3,1)=5. So first take=5+5=10. Skip: knapsack(1,5,original_copies[1])=0. Max=10.

examples.input: i=0, cap=3, copies=1, weights=[4], values=[10], n=1
examples.trace: Call knapsack(0,3,1). Base: i=0<1, cap=3>0. copies=1>0 but weight=4>3, so else: return knapsack(1,3,original_copies[1]). i=1>=n=1, base return 0.

testCases.input: i=0, cap=0, copies=1, weights=[1], values=[5], n=1
i=0, cap=1, copies=1, weights=[1], values=[5], n=1
i=0, cap=2, copies=2, weights=[1], values=[5], n=1
i=0, cap=2, copies=0, weights=[1], values=[5], n=1
i=0, cap=3, copies=2, weights=[2], values=[3], n=1
i=0, cap=1, copies=2, weights=[2], values=[3], n=1
i=0, cap=4, copies=3, weights=[1], values=[2], n=1
i=0, cap=5, copies=1, weights=[3], values=[7], n=1
i=0, cap=5, copies=2, weights=[3], values=[7], n=1
i=0, cap=2, copies=2, weights=[3], values=[7], n=1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Branching Recursion for Combinatorial Exploration
ladders.desc: Understand the recursive branching that explores include/exclude decisions for each item copy.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental recursive branching mechanism that allows exploration of all possible combinations in the bounded knapsack. The core logic is: at each step, we have two choices—include a copy of the current item or exclude it and move on. This creates a binary decision tree where each node represents a state (current item, remaining capacity, remaining copies), and edges represent decisions. The intuition is that by recursively exploring both branches, we cover the entire solution space, ensuring that no potential combination is missed. The step-by-step process: start from the first item with full copies. At each call, check if we can include a copy (based on remaining copies and weight). If yes, create a branch where we include it (update value, reduce capacity and copies) and recurse. Simultaneously, create a branch where we skip to the next item (reset to next item's copies). This branching continues until base cases are hit (no items left or no capacity). The recursion naturally backtracks to explore all paths. Time complexity arises from the exponential number of branches—each copy can lead to two recursive calls, so O(2^n). Space complexity is linear in recursion depth. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the recursive function's decision-making. Connection to L3: It relies on understanding recursion and state management. Common mistakes: incorrect branching conditions, leading to missed combinations or infinite loops. Real code pattern: the recursive function structure with two recursive calls in the take/skip decision.

examples.output: Tree exploration leading to max value 6
examples.trace: Start state: (item0, cap=4, copies=2). Branch 1: take copy: value=3, new state (item0, cap=2, copies=1). From here: take copy: value=3, state (item0, cap=0, copies=0) -> base return 0, so value=3+3=6. Skip: state (item1, cap=2, copies?) but no next item, so return 0. Max=6. Branch 2: skip: state (item1, cap=4, copies?) -> return 0. Overall max=6.

examples.output: Tree exploration leading to max value 5
examples.trace: Start: (item0, cap=1, copies=1). Branch take: value=5, state (item0, cap=0, copies=0) -> return 0, so value=5. Branch skip: state (item1, cap=1, copies?) -> return 0. Max=5.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursion with State Parameters
ladders.desc: Learn to implement recursion that tracks item index, remaining capacity, and remaining copies.
ladders.explanation: This level teaches how to set up a recursive function with multiple state parameters to solve combinatorial problems. The key idea is to define a function that takes the current state as parameters: the index of the item being considered, the remaining weight capacity, and the number of copies left for that item. The intuition is that recursion allows us to break down the problem into smaller subproblems by making decisions and updating the state accordingly. At each recursive call, we either include an item (decrementing copies and capacity) or exclude it (moving to the next item and resetting copies). The base cases handle when no more items can be considered or capacity is exhausted. Step-by-step: define function f(i, cap, copies). If i is beyond the last item or cap <= 0, return 0. Otherwise, if copies > 0 and item weight <= cap, compute include = value[i] + f(i, cap - weight[i], copies - 1) and exclude = f(i+1, cap, original_copies[i+1]), return max(include, exclude). If copies == 0 or weight > cap, return f(i+1, cap, original_copies[i+1]). Time complexity depends on the number of states explored, but here it's exponential. Space complexity is the depth of recursion. Connection to L2: This building block enables the branching logic. Connection to L4: It relies on basic recursive calls and conditionals. Common mistakes: incorrect parameter updates, leading to wrong state transitions. Real code pattern: def f(i, cap, copies): if i >= n or cap <= 0: return 0; if copies > 0 and weights[i] <= cap: return max(values[i] + f(i, cap-weights[i], copies-1), f(i+1, cap, original_copies[i+1])); else: return f(i+1, cap, original_copies[i+1]).

examples.input: i=0, cap=5, copies=2, weights=[2], values=[3], n=1, original_copies=[2]
examples.trace: Call f(0,5,2). Base false. copies=2>0 and weight=2<=5. include = 3 + f(0,3,1). Recursive f(0,3,1): include = 3 + f(0,1,0). f(0,1,0): copies=0, so else: f(1,1,original_copies[1]) but i=1>=n=1, return 0. So include=3+0=3. exclude = f(1,3,original_copies[1])=0. max=3. Thus f(0,3,1)=3. So include=3+3=6. exclude = f(1,5,original_copies[1])=0. max=6.

examples.input: i=0, cap=2, copies=1, weights=[3], values=[4], n=1, original_copies=[1]
examples.trace: Call f(0,2,1). Base false. copies=1>0 but weight=3>2, so else: f(1,2,original_copies[1]). i=1>=n=1, return 0.

testCases.input: i=0, cap=0, copies=1, weights=[1], values=[2], n=1, original_copies=[1]
i=0, cap=1, copies=1, weights=[1], values=[2], n=1, original_copies=[1]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Recursive Function Call with Conditionals
ladders.desc: Practice writing a simple recursive function that makes decisions based on conditions.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental skill of writing a recursive function that uses conditionals to decide between different actions. The operation involves: defining a function that calls itself with updated parameters, and using if-else statements to handle different cases. The intuition is that recursion solves problems by reducing them to smaller instances of the same problem. Here, we create a function that takes a state and returns a value based on whether we can take an item or not. Step-by-step: define a function compute(i, cap). If i >= number of items or cap <= 0, return 0. Otherwise, if weight[i] <= cap, compute take = value[i] + compute(i, cap - weight[i]) and skip = compute(i+1, cap), return max(take, skip). Else, return compute(i+1, cap). This is a simplified version without quantity limits. Time complexity is O(2^n) for n items. Space complexity is O(n). Connection to L3: This basic operation is extended to include copies parameter. Connection to L5: It builds on the concept of recursion. Common mistakes: missing base cases, leading to infinite recursion, or incorrect recursive calls. Real code pattern: def compute(i, cap): if i >= n or cap <= 0: return 0; if weights[i] <= cap: return max(values[i] + compute(i, cap-weights[i]), compute(i+1, cap)); else: return compute(i+1, cap).

examples.trace: Call compute(0,3). Base false. weight=2<=3. take = 5 + compute(0,1). Recursive compute(0,1): weight=2>1, so else: compute(1,1). i=1>=n=1, return 0. So take=5+0=5. skip = compute(1,3)=0. max=5.
Call compute(0,1). Base false. weight=2>1, so else: compute(1,1). i=1>=n=1, return 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Recursion and Base Cases
ladders.desc: Learn the basics of recursion: a function calling itself with a stopping condition.
ladders.explanation: Recursion is a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve a problem by breaking it down into smaller, similar subproblems. The foundation involves two key parts: the recursive case, where the function calls itself with modified parameters, and the base case, which provides a stopping condition to prevent infinite recursion. The intuition is that many problems can be defined in terms of themselves—for example, computing factorial(n) = n * factorial(n-1), with base case factorial(0)=1. In this context, we use recursion to explore decisions: at each step, we consider including or excluding an item, and recurse on the remaining problem. Step-by-step: define a function f(x). If x meets a base condition (e.g., x <= 0), return a simple value. Otherwise, compute something using f(x-1) or similar. Time complexity depends on the number of recursive calls; space complexity is the depth of the call stack. Connection to L4: This foundation is essential for writing recursive functions with conditionals. Common mistakes: forgetting the base case, leading to stack overflow, or not reducing the problem size in recursive calls. Real code pattern: def f(x): if x <= 0: return 0; else: return x + f(x-1). For example, f(3) returns 3 + f(2) = 3 + (2 + f(1)) = 3 + 2 + (1 + f(0)) = 3+2+1+0=6.

examples.trace: Call f(3). Base: x=3>0, so recursive: return 3 + f(2). Call f(2): return 2 + f(1). Call f(1): return 1 + f(0). Call f(0): base x<=0, return 0. So f(1)=1+0=1. f(2)=2+1=3. f(3)=3+3=6.
Call f(0). Base x<=0, return 0.

approaches.name: Binary Splitting DP O(n*W*log(maxQuantity))
approaches.summary: Convert bounded knapsack to 0/1 knapsack by splitting each item's quantity into powers of two. This reduces the number of items from O(n*maxQuantity) to O(n*log(maxQuantity)), then apply standard 0/1 knapsack DP. This is the core technique for bounded knapsack optimization.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Bounded Knapsack with Binary Splitting
ladders.desc: Complete solution converting bounded knapsack to 0/1 knapsack using binary splitting to optimize quantity handling.
ladders.explanation: The bounded knapsack problem involves selecting items with limited quantities to maximize value within a weight capacity. The naive approach treats each copy as separate, leading to O(n*maxQuantity*W) complexity. Binary splitting optimizes this by representing each item's quantity as a sum of powers of two (e.g., quantity 13 as 1+4+8). This reduces the number of items from O(n*maxQuantity) to O(n*log(maxQuantity)). Each split item is treated as a separate 0/1 knapsack item with weight = baseWeight * power and value = baseValue * power. Then, standard 0/1 knapsack DP is applied using a 1D DP array dp[W+1] where dp[w] stores max value for capacity w. Iterate through split items, updating dp backwards to avoid reuse. Intuition: Any quantity can be expressed as binary, allowing efficient coverage of all possible counts. Time: O(n*W*log(maxQuantity)) from splitting and DP. Space: O(W) for the DP array. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into a complete solution. Common mistakes: Incorrect splitting (e.g., missing remainders), forward DP update causing reuse, or off-by-one errors in loops. Real code pattern: Split items into powers of two, then run 0/1 knapsack DP.

examples.trace: Step 1: Split items. Item 0: quantity 2 splits into 1*2 (weight 2, value 3) and 1*2 (weight 2, value 3). Item 1: quantity 2 splits into 1*3 (weight 3, value 4) and 1*3 (weight 3, value 4). Split list: [(2,3), (2,3), (3,4), (3,4)]. Step 2: Initialize dp[0..6] = 0. Step 3: Process split items. For (2,3): w from 6 to 2: dp[6]=max(0, dp[4]+3=3) → 3, dp[5]=max(0, dp[3]+3=3) → 3, dp[4]=3, dp[3]=3, dp[2]=3. For next (2,3): w from 6 to 2: dp[6]=max(3, dp[4]+3=6) → 6, dp[5]=max(3, dp[3]+3=6) → 6, dp[4]=max(3, dp[2]+3=6) → 6, dp[3]=max(3, dp[1]+3=3) → 3, dp[2]=max(3, dp[0]+3=3) → 3. For (3,4): w from 6 to 3: dp[6]=max(6, dp[3]+4=7) → 7, dp[5]=max(6, dp[2]+4=7) → 7, dp[4]=max(6, dp[1]+4=4) → 6, dp[3]=max(3, dp[0]+4=4) → 4. For next (3,4): w from 6 to 3: dp[6]=max(7, dp[3]+4=8) → 8, dp[5]=max(7, dp[2]+4=7) → 7, dp[4]=max(6, dp[1]+4=4) → 6, dp[3]=max(4, dp[0]+4=4) → 4. Step 4: Result dp[6] = 8.

Step 1: Split items. Item 0: quantity 3 splits into 1*1 (weight 1, value 1) and 2*1 (weight 2, value 2). Item 1: quantity 1 splits into 1*2 (weight 2, value 5). Split list: [(1,1), (2,2), (2,5)]. Step 2: Initialize dp[0..5] = 0. Step 3: Process split items. For (1,1): w from 5 to 1: dp[5]=1, dp[4]=1, dp[3]=1, dp[2]=1, dp[1]=1. For (2,2): w from 5 to 2: dp[5]=max(1, dp[3]+2=3) → 3, dp[4]=max(1, dp[2]+2=3) → 3, dp[3]=max(1, dp[1]+2=3) → 3, dp[2]=max(1, dp[0]+2=2) → 2. For (2,5): w from 5 to 2: dp[5]=max(3, dp[3]+5=8) → 8, dp[4]=max(3, dp[2]+5=7) → 7, dp[3]=max(3, dp[1]+5=6) → 6, dp[2]=max(2, dp[0]+5=5) → 5. Step 4: Result dp[5] = 8.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Splitting of Item Quantities
ladders.desc: Convert each item's quantity into a list of split items using powers of two representation.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine transforms the bounded knapsack input by splitting each item's quantity into a sum of powers of two (e.g., quantity 13 = 1 + 4 + 8). For each item with weight w, value v, and quantity q, generate split items where each split has weight = w * power, value = v * power, and power is a power of two (1, 2, 4, ... up to the largest power ≤ q). If q is not a sum of exact powers (i.e., has a remainder r after subtracting powers), include an extra split with weight = w * r, value = v * r. This reduces the number of items from O(q) to O(log q). Intuition: Any integer can be represented in binary, so splitting this way allows constructing any count from 0 to q by selecting subsets of splits. Step-by-step: For each item, initialize power = 1. While power ≤ q, add split (w*power, v*power), subtract power from q, double power. If q > 0, add split (w*q, v*q). Time: O(n*log(maxQuantity)) for splitting. Space: O(n*log(maxQuantity)) to store splits. Connection: This feeds into L0 by providing the item list for 0/1 knapsack DP. Common mistakes: Forgetting the remainder split, incorrect power doubling, or miscomputing weight/value multipliers. Real code pattern: Loop through items, inner while loop for powers, add remainder if needed.

examples.trace: Item 0: w=2, v=3, q=5. Step 1: power=1 ≤5, add split (2*1=2, 3*1=3), q=5-1=4. Step 2: power=2 ≤4, add split (2*2=4, 3*2=6), q=4-2=2. Step 3: power=4 >2, exit loop. Step 4: q=2>0, add remainder split (2*2=4, 3*2=6) → wait, correction: remainder uses original q=2, so split (2*2=4, 3*2=6) but this duplicates previous? Let's recompute: Start q=5. power=1: add (2,3), q=4. power=2: add (4,6), q=2. power=4 >2, stop. q=2>0, add (2*2=4, 3*2=6) → this gives (4,6) again, which is wrong because remainder should be based on remaining q=2, but weight=2*2=4, value=3*2=6. Actually, the splits are: (2,3) for 1 copy, (4,6) for 2 copies, and since q=2 remaining, we need a split for 2 copies, which is same as previous? Let's correct: The algorithm: while power ≤ q: add (w*power, v*power), q -= power, power *= 2. Then if q>0: add (w*q, v*q). For q=5: power=1: add (2,3), q=4. power=2: add (4,6), q=2. power=4 >2, stop. q=2>0, add (2*2=4, 3*2=6). So splits: (2,3), (4,6), (4,6). But this represents 1+2+2=5 copies. Yes, that's correct: two splits of (4,6) represent 2 copies each, total 1+2+2=5. Output should be [(2,3), (4,6), (4,6)].
Item 0: w=3, v=4, q=1. Step 1: power=1 ≤1, add split (3*1=3, 4*1=4), q=1-1=0. Step 2: power=2 >0, exit loop. Step 3: q=0, no remainder. Result: [(3,4)].

ladders.title: Core Logic — 0/1 Knapsack DP with 1D Array
ladders.desc: Solve 0/1 knapsack using dynamic programming with space-optimized 1D array.
ladders.explanation: This is the standard 0/1 knapsack DP solution optimized to O(W) space. Given a list of items with weights and values, and a capacity W, we compute the maximum value achievable. Use a 1D array dp of size W+1, initialized to 0. dp[w] stores the maximum value for capacity w. Iterate through each item (weight wi, value vi). For each capacity w from W down to wi, update dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w-wi] + vi). The backward iteration ensures each item is used at most once (0/1 property). Intuition: The DP builds solutions incrementally; backward update prevents reusing the same item in the same iteration. Step-by-step: Initialize dp[0..W]=0. For each item: for w from W to wi: dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w-wi] + vi). After all items, dp[W] is the answer. Time: O(n*W). Space: O(W). Connection: This is used in L0 after binary splitting. Common mistakes: Forward iteration (causing unbounded knapsack), incorrect array bounds, or not initializing dp[0]=0. Real code pattern: Nested loops: outer for items, inner for w in reverse.

examples.trace: Initialize dp[0..3]=0. Item (1,1): w from 3 to 1: dp[3]=1, dp[2]=1, dp[1]=1. Item (2,2): w from 3 to 2: dp[3]=max(1, dp[1]+2=3) → 3, dp[2]=max(1, dp[0]+2=2) → 2. Result dp[3]=3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Representing Numbers as Sums of Powers of Two
ladders.desc: Express any integer as a sum of distinct powers of two, plus a possible remainder.
ladders.explanation: This concept underlies binary splitting: any non-negative integer q can be written as q = (2^0)*a0 + (2^1)*a1 + ... + (2^k)*ak, where each ai is 0 or 1, plus a remainder if q is not a sum of exact powers. In binary representation, this corresponds to the binary digits of q. For knapsack, we use this to split q copies into groups of sizes that are powers of two. Algorithm: Start with power = 1. While power ≤ q, include power as a group, subtract power from q, double power. If q > 0 after this, include q as a remainder group. This ensures all groups are distinct powers or the remainder. Intuition: Binary numbers allow efficient combination; e.g., to represent 5 copies, use groups of 1 and 4 (since 5 in binary is 101, meaning 1*1 + 0*2 + 1*4). Time: O(log q) to compute groups. Space: O(log q) to store them. Connection: This feeds into L1 for splitting items. Common mistakes: Incorrect power sequence, missing remainder, or confusion with binary representation. Real code pattern: While loop with power doubling.

examples.trace: Step 1: power=1 ≤13, include 1, q=13-1=12. Step 2: power=2 ≤12, include 2, q=12-2=10. Step 3: power=4 ≤10, include 4, q=10-4=6. Step 4: power=8 ≤6, include 8, q=6-8? Wait, 8 >6, so stop loop. q=6>0, include remainder 6. But groups should be distinct powers: actually, we included 1,2,4, then remainder 6, but 6 is not a power. Let's correct algorithm: Standard binary splitting uses powers until power > q, then remainder. For q=13: power=1: include 1, q=12. power=2: include 2, q=10. power=4: include 4, q=6. power=8: 8 ≤6? No, 8>6, stop. q=6>0, include 6. So groups: [1,2,4,6]. But for knapsack, we want powers of two: 13 = 1 + 4 + 8? 1+4+8=13, yes. So algorithm should be: while power ≤ q: include power, q -= power, power *= 2. Then if q>0: include q. For q=13: power=1: include 1, q=12. power=2: include 2, q=10. power=4: include 4, q=6. power=8: 8 ≤6? No, stop. q=6>0, include 6. This gives [1,2,4,6], which sums to 13. But 1+2+4+6=13, correct. However, common binary splitting for knapsack uses exact powers: 13 = 1 + 4 + 8, which is also correct. Let's use the exact powers method: Find largest power ≤ q, subtract, repeat. For q=13: largest power ≤13 is 8, include 8, q=5. Next largest power ≤5 is 4, include 4, q=1. Next largest power ≤1 is 1, include 1, q=0. So groups: [1,4,8]. I'll use this method for consistency with knapsack literature. So output: [1,4,8].
q=7. Largest power ≤7 is 4, include 4, q=3. Largest power ≤3 is 2, include 2, q=1. Largest power ≤1 is 1, include 1, q=0. Groups: [1,2,4].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Backwards Through an Array
ladders.desc: Loop through an array in reverse order to update values without overwriting needed previous states.
ladders.explanation: This operation is crucial for 0/1 knapsack DP to ensure each item is used at most once. When updating a DP array, if we iterate forwards (from low to high indices), we might reuse the same item multiple times because dp[w] could be updated based on a dp[w-wi] that already includes the current item. By iterating backwards (from high to low), we guarantee that dp[w-wi] refers to the state before considering the current item, thus enforcing the 0/1 constraint. Intuition: Think of the DP array as representing capacities; backward processing ensures that when we update dp[w], the values for lower capacities (dp[w-wi]) haven't been altered by the current item yet. Step-by-step: For an array dp of size N, and a current item with weight wi, loop for w from N-1 down to wi: update dp[w] using dp[w-wi]. Time: O(N) per item. Space: O(1) extra. Connection: This is used in L2 for 0/1 knapsack DP. Common mistakes: Incorrect loop bounds (e.g., starting at N instead of N-1), or using forward loop. Real code pattern: for w in range(capacity, wi-1, -1): dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w-wi] + vi).

examples.input: dp = [0,0,0,0,0], wi = 2, vi = 3, update in reverse
dp = [0,1,2,3,4], wi = 1, vi = 1, update in reverse

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Weights and Values in Knapsack
ladders.desc: Basic idea of selecting items with given weights and values to maximize total value under a weight limit.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept for knapsack problems. You have a set of items, each with a weight (resource cost, e.g., kilograms) and a value (benefit, e.g., dollars). You have a knapsack with a maximum weight capacity. The goal is to choose some items (each can be taken at most once in 0/1 knapsack) such that the total weight does not exceed capacity, and the total value is maximized. Intuition: It's a resource allocation problem—trade-off between weight and value. Example: If you have items: (weight 2, value 3) and (weight 3, value 4), capacity 5, you can take both (total weight 5, value 7) or just the second (weight 3, value 4), so best is both. Step-by-step: List items, consider combinations, calculate total weight and value, pick the combination with max value within weight limit. Time: O(2^n) for brute force. Space: O(1). Connection: This underpins all higher levels; binary splitting and DP are optimizations. Common mistakes: Confusing weight with value, or not checking weight constraint. Real code pattern: Simple brute force with loops or recursion.

examples.trace: Consider all subsets: 1. None: weight 0, value 0. 2. Item 0 only: weight 2, value 3. 3. Item 1 only: weight 3, value 4. 4. Both: weight 2+3=5, value 3+4=7. Valid subsets (weight ≤5): all. Max value is 7 from taking both.
Subsets: 1. None: weight 0, value 0. 2. Item 0 only: weight 1, value 1. 3. Item 1 only: weight 2, value 5. 4. Both: weight 3 >2, invalid. Valid: none, item 0, item 1. Max value is 5 from item 1 only.
